lessons.title: Locale
lessons.summary: Learn the basic usage of the java.util.Locale class, Locale constants, and Locale.Builder.

contents.title: What Is a Locale?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The role of the **Locale** [[class]] (language and region settings)
- How to create a [[Locale]] ([[constructor]], constants, builder)
- Locale.getDefault() and the system locale

The first step toward **internationalization** begins with understanding Locales.

contents.title: Locale Constants
contents.body:
The Locale class provides predefined constants for commonly used regions.
**Locale**.JAPAN represents Japan (language: ja, country: JP).
Locale.US represents the United States (language: en, country: US).
Locale.JAPANESE represents only the Japanese language and does not include country information.
Locale.ENGLISH similarly represents only the English language.
It is important to understand the difference between a language-only Locale and a language+country Locale.

contents.title: How to Create a Locale
contents.body:
There are several ways to create a Locale object.
Using constants is the simplest approach.
Using constructors is another option, but this approach is becoming deprecated.
Using **Locale**.Builder is the recommended method.
contents.note: Lines beginning with "//" are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Features of Locale.Builder
contents.body:
**Locale**.Builder allows you to safely create Locales using the builder pattern.
Use setLanguage() for the language code (ISO 639) and setRegion() for the country code (ISO 3166).
Setting an invalid value throws an IllFormedLocaleException.
The build() [[method]] produces the final Locale object.
Locale.Builder supports method chaining, offering high readability.

contents.title: Getting and Setting the Default Locale
contents.body:
**Locale**.getDefault() retrieves the current default Locale.
Locale.setDefault(Locale) changes the default Locale.
Changing the default Locale affects the entire JVM, so caution is required.
On the exam, you may be asked how changing the default Locale impacts other operations.
contents.note: **Locale**.setDefault() is a [[static method]] that affects the entire JVM.

quizzes.question: Which of the following correctly represents the language code and country code combination for Locale.JAPAN?
quizzes.explanation: Locale.JAPAN represents the combination of language code "ja" and country code "JP". Locale.JAPANESE has only the language code "ja" and does not include a country code.

quizzes.question: Which exception is thrown when an invalid language code is set in Locale.Builder?
quizzes.explanation: When an invalid value is set in Locale.Builder, an IllFormedLocaleException is thrown. This is a mechanism for safely constructing Locales.

quizzes.question:
Complete the code to set the default Locale for the entire JVM to Japan.
Locale._____(Locale.JAPAN);
quizzes.explanation: Locale.setDefault() is a static method that changes the default Locale for the entire JVM. It takes a Locale object as its argument.

lessons.title: ResourceBundle
lessons.summary: Learn about internationalization using properties files, the ResourceBundle mechanism, and fallback chains.

contents.title: What Is a ResourceBundle?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Managing locale-specific messages with **ResourceBundle**
- Properties file naming conventions (basename_ja_JP.properties)
- The [[ResourceBundle]] search order

Learn how to implement multi-language support.

contents.title: Properties File Naming Conventions
contents.body:
Properties files are named using the format basename_languageCode_countryCode.properties.
For example, messages.properties is the default, and messages_ja.properties is for Japanese.
messages_ja_JP.properties is a file for Japanese (Japan).
Files must be placed on the [[class]]path.
A file with only the base name (messages.properties) serves as the default bundle.

contents.title: How to Use ResourceBundle
contents.body:
Use **ResourceBundle**.getBundle() to obtain a bundle and getString() to retrieve the value associated with a key.
Pass the base name and a Locale to getBundle().
If the Locale is omitted, the default Locale is used.
contents.note: Lines beginning with "//" are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Fallback Chain
contents.body:
When a ResourceBundle cannot find an exact match for the specified locale, it falls back.
For example, with Locale("fr", "CA"), the search order is:
1. messages_fr_CA.properties
2. messages_fr.properties
3. messages.properties (default bundle)
If the default bundle is not found either, a MissingResourceException is thrown.
A MissingResourceException is also thrown when a key is not found.
contents.note: If a default Locale is set, bundles for the default Locale are also included in the fallback chain.

contents.title: Useful ResourceBundle Methods
contents.body:
keySet() returns all keys in the bundle as a Set<String>.
containsKey() checks whether a specific key exists.
getBaseBundleName() retrieves the base name of the bundle.
These [[methods]] are also frequently tested on the exam.

quizzes.question: When executing ResourceBundle.getBundle("app", new Locale("fr", "CA")), which properties file is searched first?
quizzes.explanation: In the fallback chain, the most specific locale file (language + country) is searched first. Therefore, app_fr_CA.properties is searched first.

quizzes.question:
Choose the output of the following code.

messages_ja.properties contains greeting=Hello.
messages.properties contains greeting=Hello and farewell=Goodbye.
quizzes.explanation: Since the farewell key does not exist in messages_ja.properties, the search falls back to the default messages.properties, retrieving "Goodbye". Key lookups fall back to the parent bundle.

quizzes.question:
Fill in the method name used to retrieve a string corresponding to a key from a ResourceBundle.
bundle._____("greeting");
quizzes.explanation: The getString() method of ResourceBundle returns the string associated with the specified key. If the key does not exist, a MissingResourceException is thrown.

quizzes.question: Complete the code for locale-aware resource handling with ResourceBundle.
quizzes.explanation: Use Locale.JAPAN to obtain the Japan locale, then use ResourceBundle.getBundle() to load the properties file.
quizzes.codeTemplate:
Locale locale = Locale.__BLANK_1__;
ResourceBundle bundle = ResourceBundle.__BLANK_2__("messages", locale);

lessons.title: NumberFormat and DateFormat
lessons.summary: Learn how to format numbers, currencies, and dates in a locale-aware manner.

contents.title: Locale-Aware Formatting
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Locale-dependent number formatting with NumberFormat and DecimalFormat
- Locale support in [[DateTimeFormatter]]
- **Internationalization** of currency formatting

Achieve appropriate display formats according to the locale.

contents.title: Using NumberFormat
contents.body:
NumberFormat.getInstance() obtains a locale-aware number formatter.
getCurrencyInstance() obtains a currency formatter.
The format() [[method]] converts a number to a string, and parse() converts a string back to a number.
contents.note: Lines beginning with "//" are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: DateTimeFormatter and Locale
contents.body:
DateTimeFormatter's ofLocalizedDate() and ofLocalizedDateTime() generate locale-aware formatters.
Use the withLocale() method to specify a Locale.
FormatStyle lets you specify the display style (FULL, LONG, MEDIUM, SHORT).
contents.note: Lines beginning with "//" are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: NumberFormat Considerations
contents.body:
The parse() method of NumberFormat can throw a ParseException.
Since parse() throws a [[checked exception]], it must be handled with try-catch or a throws declaration.
When formatting currency with getCurrencyInstance(), the appropriate currency symbol is applied based on the locale.
For Japanese yen, decimal places are truncated, while for US dollars, two decimal places are displayed.
NumberFormat is an abstract class, so you cannot create an [[instance]] with new NumberFormat().

contents.title: Exam Preparation Tips
contents.body:
On the exam, you may be asked about differences in decimal separators and grouping separators across locales.
Japan and the US use commas as grouping separators and periods as decimal separators.
Germany and France use periods as grouping separators and commas as decimal separators.
Also remember that omitting the argument to NumberFormat.getInstance() uses the default Locale.
contents.note: CompactNumberFormat is available from Java 12 onward. Check whether it is within the exam scope.

quizzes.question: Choose the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: In the German locale, periods are used as grouping separators and commas as decimal separators. Therefore, 1234.56 is displayed as "1.234,56".

quizzes.question: Which of the following is a correct way to create a NumberFormat instance?
quizzes.explanation: NumberFormat is an abstract class and cannot be instantiated using the new keyword. Use factory methods such as getInstance() or getCurrencyInstance().

quizzes.question: The following code has a compilation error. What is the cause?
quizzes.explanation: The parse() method of NumberFormat can throw a ParseException, which is a checked exception. It must be enclosed in a try-catch block or declared with a throws clause in the method signature.

choices.text: The ParseException from parse() is not handled
The argument to getCurrencyInstance() is invalid
An int type cannot be passed to format()
The return value of parse() cannot be received as a Number type
